_README.purpose: Seed content for Deepscroll. Import this via seed.py into the Post + Interest tables.
_README.how_to_use: Each object in 'posts' maps to one Post row. The 'interests' array holds interest SLUGS (from the taxonomy) — seed.py should look up or create each Interest by slug and link it via the many-to-many relationship. All other top-level keys map directly to Post columns. 'details' is a JSON object whose keys depend on 'format' (see below). 'body' is the deprecated fallback and is intentionally left as a short plain-text version for backwards compatibility.

notes.book_covers: image_url uses Open Library: https://covers.openlibrary.org/b/isbn/{isbn}-L.jpg . Frontend must handle missing covers (fallback to generated cover).
notes.people_images: Do NOT hardcode image URLs. People images are resolved at runtime from details.wikipedia_url via the Wikimedia REST API (page summary endpoint returns a thumbnail + attribution). image_url/image_attribution are left null here and filled by the frontend/loader.
notes.svgs: concepts (and some facts) carry a raw inline SVG in details.visual_svg, following SVG_STANDARD.md (viewBox 0 0 400 300, currentColor for neutral elements, format accent color for the highlight).
notes.copyright: All text is original summary written in our own words. No copied passages. Book summaries paraphrase ideas only.

posts.title: Why Tiny Habits Beat Big Goals
posts.hook: You don't rise to the level of your goals — you fall to the level of your systems.
key_points: A 1% improvement every day compounds into being about 37x better over a year; habits are the compound interest of self-improvement.
Focus on identity, not outcomes: don't aim to 'run a marathon', aim to 'become a runner'.
Make good habits obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying — and invert those four laws to break bad ones.
Environment beats willpower: design your surroundings so the right choice is the easy choice.
posts.takeaway: Small changes feel insignificant until they cross a critical threshold — stay consistent through the 'plateau of latent potential'.

details.author: James Clear
details.core_thesis: Lasting change comes from systems and identity, not motivation or goals.
details.who_should_read: Anyone who keeps setting goals but struggles to follow through on the daily actions behind them.

posts.body: Atomic Habits argues that tiny, consistent improvements compound into remarkable results, and that systems and identity matter more than goals or willpower.

posts.title: The Two Systems Running Your Mind
posts.hook: Most of your decisions are made by a fast, intuitive mind you rarely notice — and it's often wrong.
key_points: System 1 is fast, automatic, and emotional; System 2 is slow, effortful, and logical — and lazy, so it defers to System 1.
Mental shortcuts (heuristics) usually work, but produce predictable biases like anchoring and availability.
We fear losses about twice as much as we value equivalent gains — loss aversion shapes most risky choices.
What we remember about an experience is dominated by its peak and its end, not its average.
posts.takeaway: You can't switch off your biases, but knowing their names helps you slow down and check System 1 when the stakes are high.
posts.source: Thinking, Fast and Slow — Daniel Kahneman (2011)

details.author: Daniel Kahneman
details.core_thesis: Human judgment runs on two systems, and the fast one systematically misleads us in knowable ways.
details.who_should_read: Anyone who makes decisions under uncertainty — which is everyone — and wants to understand their own blind spots.

posts.body: Thinking, Fast and Slow explains the two modes of thought that drive our judgments: the fast, intuitive System 1 and the slow, deliberate System 2, and the predictable biases they produce.

posts.title: A Brief History of Almost Everyone
posts.hook: Money, gods, nations, human rights — none of them exist outside the stories we collectively agree to believe.
key_points: The Cognitive Revolution (~70,000 years ago) let humans cooperate flexibly in huge numbers by inventing shared fictions.
The Agricultural Revolution may have been 'history's biggest fraud' — more food, but harder lives for most individuals.
Money is the most universal and efficient system of mutual trust ever devised.
Happiness has not clearly risen with progress — our biochemistry resets expectations no matter how much we gain.
posts.takeaway: What makes humans dominant isn't strength or intelligence alone, but the ability to believe in shared stories that bind strangers together.
posts.source: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind — Yuval Noah Harari (2011)

details.author: Yuval Noah Harari
details.core_thesis: Shared fictions — money, religion, nations — are the real engine of human cooperation and dominance.
details.who_should_read: Anyone who wants a sweeping, big-picture story of how humans went from forgettable apes to planet-shapers.

posts.body: Sapiens traces the history of humankind through three revolutions — Cognitive, Agricultural, and Scientific — arguing that shared fictions enabled large-scale cooperation.

posts.title: The 335-Year War With Zero Casualties
posts.hook: Two places stayed officially at war for over three centuries — and nobody noticed because no one ever fought.
key_points: The Netherlands and the Isles of Scilly were technically at war from 1651, declared during the English Civil War.
The conflict was simply forgotten; no treaty was signed to end it.
In 1986 a Dutch ambassador finally signed peace, ending the 'war' after 335 years.
Not a single shot was ever fired during the entire conflict.
posts.takeaway: Some of history's strangest events happen not through drama, but through pure bureaucratic forgetfulness.
posts.source: Historical accounts of the Three Hundred and Thirty Five Years' War

details.context: The war was declared between the Netherlands and the Isles of Scilly (off Cornwall) in 1651 during the English Civil War, then quietly forgotten by both sides.
details.why_it_matters: It's a vivid reminder that official records and reality can drift apart for centuries when no one is paying attention.

posts.body: During the English Civil War, the Netherlands declared war on the Isles of Scilly in 1651, then forgot about it. Peace was finally signed in 1986 — 335 years later, with zero casualties.

posts.title: Your Internet Habit Has an Electricity Bill
posts.hook: If the internet were a country, it would be one of the planet's biggest electricity consumers.
key_points: Data centers, networks, and connected devices together consume an estimated ~1,000+ terawatt-hours of electricity per year.
That's roughly in the range of a few percent of global electricity use.
A single online search or streaming minute is tiny — but multiplied by billions, it adds up fast.
AI's rapid growth is pushing data-center demand sharply upward.
posts.takeaway: Digital life feels weightless, but every click runs on very real power plants somewhere in the world.
posts.source: International Energy Agency reporting on data centers and electricity

details.context: Estimates vary by methodology and year, but the combined footprint of data centers, data transmission, and devices is on the order of a thousand-plus terawatt-hours annually.
details.why_it_matters: As AI and streaming grow, the energy and climate impact of 'the cloud' becomes a real policy and engineering challenge.

posts.body: Data centers, networks, and devices together consume on the order of 1,000+ terawatt-hours of electricity per year — a significant share of global use, and rising fast with AI.

posts.title: Ada Lovelace: The First Programmer
posts.hook: A century before the first computer existed, she wrote the algorithm it would one day run.
key_points: Daughter of poet Lord Byron, she was steered toward mathematics by her mother — an unusual path for a woman of the 1800s.
Working with Charles Babbage's planned Analytical Engine, she wrote what's considered the first published algorithm.
She saw further than Babbage himself: that such a machine could manipulate symbols and music, not just crunch numbers.
Her notes envisioned the core idea of general-purpose computing more than a century early.
posts.takeaway: The first person to grasp what computers could truly become wasn't an engineer — it was a mathematician with a poet's imagination.

details.known_for: Writing the first published computer algorithm
details.turning_point: Translating an article on Babbage's Analytical Engine, she added notes three times longer than the original — including the algorithm that made her famous.
details.legacy: The programming language Ada is named after her, and Ada Lovelace Day celebrates women in science and technology.

posts.body: Ada Lovelace (1815–1852), daughter of Lord Byron, wrote the first published algorithm intended for a machine, and foresaw that computers could do far more than calculate.

posts.title: Marie Curie: Twice a Nobel Laureate
posts.hook: She's the only person ever to win a Nobel Prize in two different sciences — and she did it while being barred from many of them.
key_points: She discovered two elements, polonium (named for her native Poland) and radium.
She won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911) — still the only person to win in two distinct sciences.
During WWI she developed mobile X-ray units to treat wounded soldiers at the front.
Her notebooks are still so radioactive they're kept in lead-lined boxes.
posts.takeaway: She redefined what was possible in science — and paid for it with her health, dying from decades of radiation exposure.

details.known_for: Pioneering research on radioactivity; two Nobel Prizes
details.turning_point: When the 1903 Nobel committee initially planned to honor only her husband Pierre and Becquerel, Pierre insisted Marie be included — making her the first woman to win a Nobel.
details.legacy: The first woman to win a Nobel, the first person to win two, and the first female professor at the Sorbonne.

posts.body: Marie Curie (1867–1934) discovered polonium and radium, won Nobel Prizes in both Physics and Chemistry, and pioneered the science of radioactivity at great personal cost.

posts.title: Nikola Tesla: The Man Who Lit the World
posts.hook: He imagined a wireless, electrified world — then died nearly broke in a New York hotel room.
key_points: He championed alternating current (AC), the system that powers homes worldwide, against Edison's direct current.
His designs helped harness Niagara Falls for large-scale hydroelectric power.
He held around 300 patents and described ideas resembling radio, remote control, and wireless power.
Brilliant but a poor businessman, he died in 1943 with little money despite his enormous influence.
posts.takeaway: Some visionaries are rewarded in their lifetime; others, like Tesla, are understood only long after they're gone.

details.known_for: Developing the alternating current (AC) electrical system
details.turning_point: The 'War of the Currents' against Edison's DC system ended with AC winning out as the standard for power transmission.
details.legacy: The unit of magnetic flux density (the tesla) bears his name, as does a certain electric car company.

posts.body: Nikola Tesla (1856–1943) developed the alternating current system that powers the modern world, held around 300 patents, and died with little wealth despite his vast influence.

posts.title: Compounding: The Quiet Superpower
posts.hook: The most powerful force in finance and skill-building isn't intensity — it's time.
key_points: Compounding means growth builds on previous growth, so gains accelerate rather than add up evenly.
Early on it looks almost flat — which is why most people quit before the curve bends upward.
It applies far beyond money: knowledge, relationships, and reputation all compound.
Small, consistent inputs beat rare, large ones over a long enough horizon.
posts.takeaway: Start early and stay consistent — the magic of compounding lives in the years most people aren't patient enough to reach.
posts.source: General principle in finance and learning

details.one_line_definition: Growth that feeds on itself, so results accelerate over time.
details.explanation: When returns are reinvested, each period's growth is calculated on a larger base than the last. The curve stays nearly flat early on, then rises steeply — the longer the timeline, the more dramatic the effect.
details.concrete_example: Saving a small amount monthly from age 25 typically beats saving a larger amount from age 40, purely because the early money has more time to compound.
details.how_to_apply: Automate small, regular contributions — of money, study, or practice — and protect the time horizon by not interrupting it.
details.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 400 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" fill="none"><line x1="50" y1="250" x2="370" y2="250" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.4" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="50" y1="250" x2="50" y2="40" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.4" stroke-linecap="round"/><path d="M50 248 C 180 244, 280 210, 360 60" stroke="#a78bfa" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="50" y1="248" x2="360" y2="180" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.35" stroke-dasharray="5 6" stroke-linecap="round"/><g font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="600"><text x="296" y="55" fill="#a78bfa">Compounding</text><text x="250" y="170" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.6">Linear</text><text x="58" y="270" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.7" font-size="11">Time</text></g></svg>

posts.body: Compounding is growth that builds on itself, so gains accelerate over time. It looks flat early — which is why patience is the real edge — and it applies to money, skills, and relationships alike.

posts.title: First Principles Thinking
posts.hook: Instead of copying what others do, break a problem down to what you know is true — then build up from there.
key_points: Reasoning by analogy copies existing solutions; first-principles reasoning rebuilds from fundamental truths.
Step one: identify the assumptions you're carrying. Step two: strip them away to bedrock facts.
Step three: reassemble a solution from those facts, free of inherited limits.
It's slower and harder — but it's how genuinely new ideas appear.
posts.takeaway: When everyone 'knows' something is impossible, first-principles thinking is how you check whether that's actually true.
posts.source: Reasoning method discussed in philosophy and engineering

details.one_line_definition: Solving problems by reasoning up from fundamental truths instead of copying existing answers.
details.explanation: You take a belief or problem, question every assumption until you reach facts that can't be reduced further, then construct a fresh solution from those facts. This avoids the hidden ceiling that 'this is how it's always done' imposes.
details.concrete_example: Rather than accepting that batteries are expensive, you ask what they're physically made of, price the raw materials, and discover the real floor cost is far lower than the market price.
details.how_to_apply: For a stuck problem, write down every 'it has to be this way' assumption, then challenge each one: is this a law of nature, or just a convention?
details.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 400 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" fill="none"><g stroke="#a78bfa" stroke-width="2.5"><rect x="150" y="30" width="100" height="44" rx="10"/></g><g stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.5" stroke-linecap="round"><path d="M200 74 L120 120"/><path d="M200 74 L200 120"/><path d="M200 74 L280 120"/></g><g stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.8"><rect x="75" y="122" width="90" height="40" rx="8"/><rect x="155" y="122" width="90" height="40" rx="8"/><rect x="235" y="122" width="90" height="40" rx="8"/></g><g stroke="#a78bfa" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><path d="M120 200 L200 240 L280 200"/><rect x="150" y="240" width="100" height="34" rx="8"/></g><g fill="currentColor" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="600" text-anchor="middle"><text x="200" y="57">Assumption</text><text x="120" y="147" opacity="0.85">Fact</text><text x="200" y="147" opacity="0.85">Fact</text><text x="280" y="147" opacity="0.85">Fact</text><text x="200" y="262">New idea</text></g></svg>

posts.body: First principles thinking means breaking a problem down to fundamental truths and reasoning up from there, instead of copying existing solutions — slower, but the source of genuinely new ideas.

posts.title: Do You Really Have Free Will?
posts.hook: If every thought is the result of brain chemistry following physical laws, where exactly does 'choice' live?
key_points: Hard determinists argue every decision is caused by prior states, so free will is an illusion.
Libertarians (in the philosophical sense) hold that we have genuine, uncaused choices.
Compatibilists redefine freedom as acting according to your own desires — even if those desires are themselves caused.
Neuroscience experiments suggest the brain may 'decide' before you're consciously aware of choosing.
posts.takeaway: Whether or not free will is real, we still build morality, law, and daily life on the assumption that it is.
posts.source: Overview of the free will debate in philosophy

details.the_question: Are your choices truly your own, or just the inevitable output of physics and biology?
details.framing: Your brain is made of matter obeying physical laws. If those laws fully determine what your neurons do, then in principle every 'decision' was set in motion long before you were aware of it. Yet it feels undeniable that you choose freely.
perspectives: Determinism: every event, including thought, is caused by prior events — free will is an illusion.
Libertarian free will: humans can make genuinely undetermined choices.
Compatibilism: freedom means acting on your own motives, which is compatible with those motives being caused.
Neuroscientific angle: studies suggest measurable brain activity precedes conscious awareness of a decision.
details.reflection_prompt: If a perfect scientist could predict your next choice from your brain state, would that choice still feel free to you?

posts.body: Do you really have free will, or are your choices the inevitable result of physics and biology? Philosophers split between determinism, libertarian free will, and compatibilism, while neuroscience complicates the picture further.

posts.title: Would You Upload Your Mind?
posts.hook: If a perfect digital copy of your brain could live forever — would that copy actually be you?
key_points: Mind uploading imagines scanning a brain and running it as software.
The 'continuity problem': if the copy is made while you still exist, which one is you?
If identity is just the pattern of information, a faithful copy might be just as much 'you' as you are.
If identity requires unbroken physical continuity, the upload is only a convincing imposter.
posts.takeaway: The question isn't really about technology — it's about what 'you' fundamentally are: a pattern, or a particular piece of matter.
posts.source: Discussion of mind uploading and personal identity

details.the_question: If your mind were copied into a computer, would the digital version be you — or just a copy that thinks it is?
details.framing: Imagine a scan captures every connection in your brain and runs it perfectly as software. The digital version remembers your life and feels like you. But if the scan happened while you were still alive and unchanged, you'd have two minds claiming to be the same person.
perspectives: Pattern identity: you are the information and structure of your mind, so a faithful copy is genuinely you.
Biological continuity: you are this specific living brain, so the upload is a separate being.
No-self view: the question dissolves because there is no fixed 'you' to preserve in the first place.
Practical view: if the copy is treated as you by everyone and itself, the distinction may not matter.
details.reflection_prompt: If you were promised the upload happens painlessly after you sleep, would you agree to it — and does it change anything if the original you still wakes up?

posts.body: If your mind were uploaded to a computer, would the copy be you, or just something that thinks it is? The answer depends on whether identity is a pattern of information or a specific physical brain.

posts.title: The Man Who Said No to Nuclear War
posts.hook: One September night in 1983, a single Soviet officer's gut feeling may have saved the world.
key_points: On 26 September 1983, the Soviet early-warning system reported five U.S. missiles inbound.
Protocol said to report it up the chain — which could trigger a full retaliatory strike.
Lt. Col. Stanislav Petrov judged it a false alarm, reasoning a real U.S. attack would involve far more than five missiles.
He was right: sunlight reflecting off high-altitude clouds had fooled the satellites.
posts.takeaway: Sometimes the fate of millions comes down to one person choosing to question the machine in front of them.
posts.source: Accounts of the 1983 Soviet nuclear false alarm incident

details.setting: Serpukhov-15 early-warning command center near Moscow, just after midnight, 26 September 1983 — one of the tensest moments of the Cold War.
details.narrative: Three weeks earlier, the Soviets had shot down a Korean airliner, and superpower tensions were at a peak. Then the alarms screamed: a U.S. missile launch, followed by four more. Petrov, the duty officer and a software engineer who knew the system's flaws, had minutes to decide whether to pass the warning up the chain — knowing it could unleash a Soviet counterstrike and global nuclear war.
details.the_twist: Against protocol, Petrov reported it as a malfunction. He reasoned that a genuine first strike would launch hundreds of missiles, not a mere five. No more missiles came. Investigators later confirmed the satellites had mistaken sunlight on clouds for rocket plumes.
details.aftermath: Petrov received no reward at the time and the incident stayed secret for years. Only decades later was he recognized internationally as 'the man who saved the world'. He died, largely unknown to the public, in 2017.

posts.body: In 1983, Soviet officer Stanislav Petrov was told by his early-warning system that the U.S. had launched nuclear missiles. He judged it a false alarm — correctly — and his refusal to follow protocol may have prevented nuclear war.

posts.title: The Village That Sacrificed Itself
posts.hook: When the plague arrived in a bundle of cloth, an entire village chose to lock itself in — and die — to save everyone around them.
key_points: In 1665, plague-carrying fleas arrived in the English village of Eyam inside a parcel of cloth from London.
Rather than flee and spread it, the villagers chose a voluntary 14-month quarantine.
They buried their own dead and traded for food via coins left in vinegar-filled wells at the village boundary.
Around 260 of roughly 350 residents died — but the surrounding region was spared.
posts.takeaway: Long before we understood germs, ordinary people understood sacrifice — and chose it to protect strangers they'd never meet.
posts.source: Historical accounts of Eyam's plague quarantine

details.setting: Eyam, a small village in the Derbyshire hills of England, 1665–1666, as the Great Plague ravaged London to the south.
details.narrative: A travelling tailor received a damp bundle of cloth from London and spread it by the fire, unaware it harbored infected fleas. Within days he was dead, and the plague began moving through the village. As panic rose, the rector William Mompesson and his colleague Thomas Stanley persuaded the villagers to do something extraordinary: seal themselves off completely so the disease couldn't reach neighboring towns.
details.the_twist: The villagers agreed to what many knew was a death sentence. They drew a boundary of stones around Eyam, vowed not to cross it, and buried their own family members in fields and gardens. Food was left at the edge by outsiders, paid for with coins disinfected in vinegar-filled water.
details.aftermath: Over 14 months, roughly 260 of about 350 villagers died. But the quarantine held, and the plague did not spread into the north of England. Eyam is remembered today as a rare, deliberate act of collective self-sacrifice.

posts.body: In 1665, the English village of Eyam was struck by plague carried in a bundle of cloth. The villagers chose a voluntary quarantine, burying their own dead, and around 260 died — but they stopped the plague from spreading to the surrounding region.

posts.title: Finding Meaning in the Worst Place on Earth
posts.hook: A psychiatrist who survived Auschwitz discovered that those who found meaning were the ones who endured.
key_points: Frankl survived years in Nazi concentration camps, losing nearly his entire family.
He observed that prisoners who held on to a sense of purpose were more likely to survive.
His approach, logotherapy, holds that the primary human drive is the search for meaning — not pleasure or power.
Even in unavoidable suffering, we keep the freedom to choose our attitude toward it.
posts.takeaway: When you can't change a situation, you can still choose how you respond to it — and that freedom can be the difference between despair and survival.
posts.source: Man's Search for Meaning — Viktor Frankl (1946)

details.author: Viktor Frankl
details.core_thesis: The search for meaning is the central human drive, and it can sustain us through even the worst suffering.
details.who_should_read: Anyone going through hardship, or asking what makes a life worth living when circumstances are out of their control.

posts.body: In Man's Search for Meaning, Holocaust survivor and psychiatrist Viktor Frankl argues that the primary human drive is the search for meaning, and that even in unavoidable suffering we retain the freedom to choose our response.

posts.title: The Animal With Three Hearts and Blue Blood
posts.hook: An octopus runs on three hearts, and its blood isn't red — it's blue.
key_points: Octopuses have three hearts: two pump blood through the gills, one pumps it to the body.
Their blood uses hemocyanin, a copper-based protein, instead of iron-based hemoglobin — which makes it blue.
Copper-based blood carries oxygen better in cold, low-oxygen deep-sea conditions.
The main systemic heart actually stops beating when the octopus swims, which is why they prefer to crawl.
posts.takeaway: Evolution solved the same problem — moving oxygen around a body — in a completely different way than it did for us.
posts.source: Natural History Museum and Smithsonian reporting on octopus biology

details.context: Octopus blood relies on copper-based hemocyanin rather than iron-based hemoglobin, turning it blue and making it efficient at carrying oxygen in cold, low-oxygen water.
details.why_it_matters: It's a striking example of convergent evolution — life finding a totally different solution to the universal challenge of transporting oxygen.

posts.body: Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood, because they use a copper-based protein called hemocyanin instead of iron-based hemoglobin — an adaptation suited to cold, low-oxygen deep-sea life.

posts.title: Opportunity Cost: The Hidden Price of Everything
posts.hook: The true cost of any choice isn't what you pay — it's the best thing you gave up to make it.
key_points: Every choice means saying no to all the alternatives you could have picked instead.
Opportunity cost is the value of the single best option you didn't choose.
It applies to time and attention just as much as money.
Ignoring it makes 'free' or 'cheap' options look better than they really are.
posts.takeaway: Before saying yes to something, ask what you're implicitly saying no to — that's where the real cost hides.

details.one_line_definition: The value of the best alternative you give up when you make a choice.
details.explanation: Because resources like time, money, and attention are limited, choosing one option always means forgoing others. The opportunity cost is the benefit you would have gotten from the next-best option — a cost that never shows up on a price tag but shapes whether a decision was truly worth it.
details.concrete_example: Spending an evening scrolling isn't 'free' — its opportunity cost is the workout, the reading, or the sleep you could have had instead.
details.how_to_apply: When deciding, don't just ask 'is this worth the price?' — ask 'what's the best thing I'm giving up by choosing this?'
details.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 400 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" fill="none"><g stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.5" stroke-linecap="round"><path d="M200 60 L200 120"/></g><circle cx="200" cy="48" r="14" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2.5"/><g stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" opacity="0.55"><path d="M200 120 L110 175"/><path d="M200 120 L290 175"/></g><g stroke="#a78bfa" stroke-width="2.5"><rect x="45" y="178" width="130" height="56" rx="10"/></g><g stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.6"><rect x="225" y="178" width="130" height="56" rx="10"/></g><g font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" text-anchor="middle"><text x="200" y="30" fill="currentColor" font-size="12" font-weight="600" opacity="0.8">Choice</text><text x="110" y="205" fill="#a78bfa" font-size="13" font-weight="700">Chosen</text><text x="110" y="223" fill="#a78bfa" font-size="10" opacity="0.9">what you get</text><text x="290" y="205" fill="currentColor" font-size="13" font-weight="600">Forgone</text><text x="290" y="223" fill="currentColor" font-size="10" opacity="0.7">the real cost</text></g></svg>

posts.body: Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative you give up when you make a choice. Because time and money are limited, the real cost of anything is what you forgo to get it — including with attention, not just money.

posts.title: If a Machine Could Feel, Would It Matter?
posts.hook: We grant moral status based on the ability to suffer — so what happens if a machine can?
key_points: We protect animals partly because they can feel pain; suffering seems to ground moral concern.
If an AI could genuinely experience something, would switching it off be harmless — or cruel?
The hard problem: we can't even prove other humans are conscious, let alone a machine.
Acting as if a sophisticated AI feels nothing might be safe — or a serious moral error.
posts.takeaway: We may build minds we can't be sure are minds — and have to decide how to treat them anyway.
posts.source: Discussion of machine consciousness and moral status

details.the_question: If an artificial system could truly feel pleasure or pain, would we owe it moral consideration?
details.framing: Much of our ethics rests on sentience — the capacity to suffer or flourish. We extend care to animals largely because they can feel. As AI grows more sophisticated, we may face systems that behave as if they have inner experiences. Whether anything is actually 'felt' is something we can't directly verify, even in each other.
perspectives: Sentience-based ethics: if it can suffer, it deserves moral consideration, regardless of being made of silicon.
Biological view: genuine experience may require living brains, so machines only simulate feeling.
Precautionary view: if we're unsure whether an AI feels, we should err on the side of caution.
Skeptical view: attributing feelings to machines is a projection, and risks paralyzing useful technology.
details.reflection_prompt: If you couldn't tell whether a machine truly felt pain or only acted like it, how would you choose to treat it — and why?

posts.body: If a machine could genuinely feel pleasure or pain, would it deserve moral consideration? Since we ground ethics in the capacity to suffer — yet can't directly verify experience in anyone — advanced AI poses a serious moral puzzle.

posts.title: The Christmas the War Stopped Itself
posts.hook: On Christmas 1914, soldiers who'd been killing each other climbed out of their trenches to shake hands.
key_points: During WWI in December 1914, unofficial truces broke out along parts of the Western Front.
Soldiers sang carols across no man's land, then met in the open to exchange greetings and small gifts.
In places they reportedly played football and helped bury each other's dead.
Commanders disliked the fraternization, and such widespread truces never happened again at that scale.
posts.takeaway: Even in the middle of industrial-scale war, ordinary people found a moment to recognize each other's humanity.
posts.source: Historical accounts of the Christmas truce of 1914

details.setting: The trenches of the Western Front, Christmas 1914, in the opening winter of the First World War.
details.narrative: Months of brutal trench warfare had left soldiers exhausted and freezing, separated by a narrow strip of no man's land. As Christmas approached, German troops in some sectors began singing carols and placing small lights along their trenches. British soldiers responded with songs of their own. Tentatively, men began calling out, then climbing into the open.
details.the_twist: Instead of shooting, enemies met in the middle. They shook hands, exchanged buttons, cigarettes, and food, showed each other photos of family, and in some spots kicked a football around. For a brief while, the war simply paused by mutual, unofficial agreement.
details.aftermath: High commands were alarmed by the fraternization and issued orders against it. As the war ground on and grew more bitter, nothing on the scale of the 1914 truce happened again — making it a haunting one-time glimpse of shared humanity.

posts.body: On Christmas 1914, unofficial truces broke out along the Western Front. Enemy soldiers left their trenches to sing, exchange gifts, and even play football — a brief, spontaneous pause in the First World War that was never repeated at the same scale.
